title: Support data volume containers as init containers in podman kube
body:
Data volume containers are created as init containers with a new type "idle" using annotation. Data volume containers do not have runtime execution binaries. Such containers are not started.

Related to: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/16819

<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

In case you're only changing docs, make sure to prefix the pull-request title with "[CI:DOCS]". That will prevent functional tests from running and save time and energy.

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name. Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change? Yes

<!--
If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note
is required: Enter your extended release note in the block below. If the PR
requires additional action from users switching to the new release, include the
string "action required".

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

```release-note
The init container supports a new type "idle". The idle init containers are created but not run. This type of init container is useful in use-cases such as data volume containers that do not have runtime execution environment.
```

Is there a reason to implement these as separate containers, instead of adding the volumes to the existing infra container?

> Is there a reason to implement these as separate containers, instead of adding the volumes to the existing infra container?

This is on the existing pattern of defining volumes and attaching them to specific containers in the pod.

So... Is the ask here for a way to have multiple sets of volumes that a container can get by inheriting from one or more containers?

I'm very dubious about the use of init containers for this; it doesn't feel like a proper use of init containers, which are meant to run (at least once). Is there a reason a normal container running `sleep inf` can't do the same job?

Instead of changing the supported values for `persistentVolumeClaim` which is a k8s native field in the yaml, I would recommend creating another annotation, something like ` io.podman.annotations.volumes.from` that then does the whole set up of volume mounts from the container specified.

We want to ensure we don't change the allowed syntax of a k8s yaml file for podman specific use case, that is what the annotations is there for.

> So... Is the ask here for a way to have multiple sets of volumes that a container can get by inheriting from one or more containers?
> 
> I'm very dubious about the use of init containers for this; it doesn't feel like a proper use of init containers, which are meant to run (at least once). Is there a reason a normal container running `sleep inf` can't do the same job?

The volumesFrom change is in this PR is not limited to init containers. It could be any container. The way how CLI works.

As far as my use-case of init container volumes, it is about data volumes containers that do not have any runtime execution environment. So, I introduced the "idle" type.

> Instead of changing the supported values for `persistentVolumeClaim` which is a k8s native field in the yaml, I would recommend creating another annotation, something like ` io.podman.annotations.volumes.from` that then does the whole set up of volume mounts from the container specified.
> 
> We want to ensure we don't change the allowed syntax of a k8s yaml file for podman specific use case, that is what the annotations is there for.

There can be several volumes-from containers. The annotation approach can handle one unless we make the annotation value a CSV or list.

The k8s yaml stays intact. Podman is extending the use-case as it does using annotations and other approaches.

@mheon ,
Looking for a response whether it is okay to continue with the current approach or is there a strong push back?

We don't want to have podman specific syntax in the k8s fields that either won't work with k8s or will be ignored by k8s. Podman specific options/changes should be done via annotations only.

The annotation can take a comma separated stringt, so the `volumes.from` annotation can take a string of container names separated by a comma or some other character and we can break those apart to a list to be used as volumes for running containers.

One more thing to consider is there could be multiple containers in a pod, so maybe have the `volumes.from` syntax be something like `src-ctr:dest-ctr` so we know which container we want to mount those volumes to.

> We don't want to have podman specific syntax in the k8s fields that either won't work with k8s or will be ignored by k8s. Podman specific options/changes should be done via annotations only.
> 
> The annotation can take a comma separated stringt, so the `volumes.from` annotation can take a string of container names separated by a comma or some other character and we can break those apart to a list to be used as volumes for running containers.
> 
> One more thing to consider is there could be multiple containers in a pod, so maybe have the `volumes.from` syntax be something like `src-ctr:dest-ctr` so we know which container we want to mount those volumes to.

Use of annotations will mean not only the `from` & `to` containers but also the supported mount options.

Noticed that Podman pod already supports an annotation [io.podman.annotations.volumes-from](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/libpod/define/annotations.go#L27) that was added via https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/11777.

So, changing the approach to add the support in podman kube YAML.

What I am not sure about this pod annotation is that the value can be comma separated containers in _container:[mount-options]_ format. See https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/libpod/pod.go#L281. The mount options can be `ro,z`. In this case, the parsing is going to result in an unexpected behavior.

For kube annotation, the field separator will need to be a character that cannot appear in the container name and mount options. For example, semi-colon (`;`).

Yet to see what happens when a container name that is specified within the pod is mentioned as `volumes-from` annotation.

As I suspected, the current field separator `,` does have an issue. When a `volumes-from` value is `ctr1:z,ro`, the container inspect shows the following:
```
               "VolumesFrom": [
                    "f91dc2fa04d6cd3997c7b3f5c300ceca39bc37e5c5dbb847e89d342d8141c663:z",
                    "ro"
               ],
```

This indicates the internal field separator will need to change from `,` to maybe `;`.
Created https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/21497 for the inspect annotation field separator issue.
Have moved the volume mount logic out of this PR.  Will create a separate PR for that. This PR specifically adds support for init container type "idle".

> Have moved the volume mount logic out of this PR. Will create a separate PR for that. This PR specifically adds support for init container type "idle".

@mheon , @TomSweeneyRedHat , @rhatdan , please review

@ygalblum PTAL
Is your intention to support having data in a volume in a container but never running it?

Could I have a Containerfile like

from scratch
VOLUME /
ADD localdata /

And then use this for a Volume container and mount it under /data?

I am looking for a way to ship data as a volume and then mount it into a container when running in k8s?

Specifically looking at AI Models?

> @ygalblum PTAL Is your intention to support having data in a volume in a container but never running it?
> 
> Could I have a Containerfile like
> 
> from scratch VOLUME / ADD localdata /
> 
> And then use this for a Volume container and mount it under /data?
> 
> I am looking for a way to ship data as a volume and then mount it into a container when running in k8s?
> 
> Specifically looking at AI Models?

The Containerfile content is very close. The VOLUME can be a list of volumes at different locations, say `["/opt/vendor/tool1", "/opt/vendor/plugin1"]`. These volumes will get mounted to the same location inside the containers as specified in the Containerfile.
The changes LGTM in general.   The "machine-mac podman drawing rootless host sqlite" test failed.  I suspect it's a flake and I did restart
@Luap99 Thoughts?

I have a hard time understanding the use case, is the only goal of the idle container to be used with volumes-from? I do not see why a container is needed for that? Couldn't you just specify the same volume for each container in the yaml?
Podman already supports image volumes (`--mount type=image,src=quay.io/libpod/testimage:20221018,dst=/test`) so there is really no need to have a dummy container here. Also `podman volume create --driver image --opt image=quay.io/libpod/testimage:20221018 test` is supported. If this doesn't work via the yaml then I think we should be looking at supporting this there. I assume this is what you need for your use case?
The use cases are mentioned in the issue that this PR fixes. Also, replied there that image as a volume does not help.

Ok, I see what you mean but I still do not get why we would want to create a "useless" container for this. To me the most logical would be to allow --volumes-from to specify directly an image and then it uses all the VOLUME definitions from that image.

I agree that using an image for this would match up to `--mount type=image` functionality.
Basically I want to mount an entire image as a volume for AI Models.
The suggestion from @Luap99 about `--volumes-from` seems solid to me, though maybe a separate flag? `--volumes-from-image`?
I definitely feel like containers that never start is not the correct approach.

The `--volumes-from-image` would make sense if it existed.

This PR is introducing a kube change for something that one can do using podman CLI. There is no container level new functionality. The "idle" container provides the value for data volume container and choice of using this type can be left to the user. By adding the kube support, it helps podman consumers move to `podman kube` and simplify the deployment.

After consulting with the team, we agree the idea is solid, but we don't feel this is a good implementation - this is not a good fit for init containers functionality. We're looking into adding something similar for our own tools (hence the comments from @rhatdan) and we are going to be discussing details at the next Podman Community Cabal on the 20th of this month to come to a final conclusion on next steps. If you could join that, we can incorporate your requirements into that work.

Closing this for now as we don't think this implementation is the path forward.
We have agreed to consider the following model https://github.com/kubernetes-retired/csi-driver-image-populator for specifying kube.YAML.

> We have agreed to consider the following model https://github.com/kubernetes-retired/csi-driver-image-populator for specifying kube.YAML.

Scenario:
A data image can have multiple volumes defined in its Containerfile, say `VOLUMES ["/path1/to/vol1", "/path2/to/vol2", "/path2/to/vol3"]`. All the defined volumes should get mounted inside specified container at the exact respective locations.
